text.title: ab-testing-implementation Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for ab-testing-implementation. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Model every experiment event as a typed dataclass with experiment_id, variant, user_id, event_type (exposure | conversion | metric), metric_name, metric_value, timestamp, and properties.
rule.rationale: An untyped dict passed to the analytics client drifts over time; a dataclass is a code-reviewable schema that fails fast when fields are missing.

rule.statement: Deduplicate exposures by (experiment_id, user_id) within a request/session; never fire multiple exposure events for the same user in the same experiment during one page load.
rule.rationale: Duplicate exposures inflate the exposure count, causing sample-ratio mismatch and underestimating conversion rate.

rule.statement: In-process deduplication (_exposures set) is for development only; in production, persist the deduplication state to a durable store (Redis or a warehouse exposure table).
rule.rationale: In-process sets are lost on pod restart; in a multi-replica deployment each pod builds its own set, allowing duplicates across replicas.

rule.statement: Track exposure when the variant is rendered, not when assignment happens; track conversion on the specific downstream event — never at the same call site as exposure.
rule.rationale: Logging exposure and conversion at the same site conflates assignment with outcome. Analysis must join exposure → subsequent conversion; co-located calls destroy the temporal relationship.

rule.statement: Refuse to run analysis unless: minimum runtime has elapsed, pre-registered sample size has been reached, and SRM check passes (chi-square p > 0.001).
rule.rationale: Running analysis before sample size is reached inflates false positives. SRM (sample-ratio mismatch) indicates a bucketing or logging bug; analysis on SRM data produces biased lift estimates.

rule.statement: Use a two-sided z-test for binary conversion metrics; compute pooled standard error from combined conversion rate; report Wilson confidence intervals (not Wald intervals) for robustness at small n.
rule.rationale: Wald intervals can include values outside [0,1] for very high or very low rates; Wilson intervals are bounded and more accurate for proportions near 0 or 1.

rule.statement: Maintain a typed metric registry: binary (z-test for proportions), continuous (Welch's t-test), ratio (delta method) — never apply the binary z-test to continuous or ratio metrics.
rule.rationale: Applying a binomial z-test to continuous metrics (e.g., revenue per user) produces wrong variance estimates and unreliable p-values.

rule.statement: Analysis output must be structured JSON: control stats, treatment stats, lift, p_value, confidence_interval, power, srm_p, duration_days, decision_ready boolean — never a narrative sentence like "the variant is winning".
rule.rationale: A narrative sentence encodes a decision; the structured output presents evidence. Humans decide ship/no-ship; the analyzer presents the evidence without editorializing.

rule.statement: Expose experiment summaries as a JSON endpoint with per-variant exposure counts, conversion rates, analysis results, and a generated_at timestamp; never cache summary data beyond 5 minutes to reflect new conversions.
rule.rationale: A stale cached summary misleads operators checking experiment health; the 5-minute window balances freshness against repeated heavy analysis queries.

rule.statement: Provide daily metric data for at least the last 14 days; include both cumulative and per-day rates to detect novelty effects (initial spike that fades) or ramp effects (gradual improvement).
rule.rationale: Cumulative-only reporting hides temporal patterns. A novelty effect produces an early significant result that regresses to null later; per-day data reveals this before shipping.

rule.statement: Monitor at least two guardrail metrics alongside the primary metric: latency p95 and error rate; flag any guardrail degradation even if the primary metric is positive.
rule.rationale: A checkout conversion win that comes with +200ms latency and +0.5pp error rate may not be a net positive for the business; guardrails catch these regressions before shipping.
